hero.tagline: Where robots and humans share space, intent, and trust
hero.intro: A robot in a lab is an engineering problem. A robot near a person is a different problem - now you have to think about safety, predictability, communication, and trust. Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) is the design discipline behind shared autonomy, natural-language commanding (now LLM-powered), gesture recognition, the safety standards that let cobots work next to humans without fences, teleoperation rigs (which double as training-data factories), haptic feedback, and the social robots people see most. If you're building something that touches a human - directly or indirectly - read this.

sections.heading: What HRI is - and isn't
sections.body:
HRI is the discipline of making robots usable by humans. It's distinct from robotics-engineering disciplines (perception, planning, control) which focus on what the robot can do; HRI focuses on the closed loop with the human in it. Done well, you barely notice it: the robot signals intent, the human stays comfortable, the task gets done. Done poorly, you get the uncanny valley, frustrating UX, or - at the safety extreme - actual injury.

HRI is also distinct from teleoperation (which is one tool inside HRI). Teleop is one operator driving one robot; HRI scales to ambient interactions, multi-user supervisory control, and fully-autonomous robots whose behavior must still be legible to bystanders.
bullets: Different from perception/planning - the human is in the loop, not just an obstacle
Different from teleop - teleop is one mode within the broader HRI design space
Different from UX - physical robots add safety, embodiment, latency, presence problems UX doesn't
Major venues: ACM/IEEE HRI conference (March), RO-MAN (Aug), CHI (May)
Notable labs: MIT Media Lab, CMU HARP, Stanford IRIS, Disney Research, Furhat / SoftBank

sections.heading: Shared autonomy - splitting decisions human ↔ robot
sections.body: Pure teleop is exhausting; full autonomy is risky. Most real systems sit in between - shared autonomy - where the human supplies high-level intent and the robot fills in the low-level execution. Variants: supervisory control (human approves the robot's plan before execution), trading control (control swaps between human and robot at handoff points), shared blended (human and robot inputs are mixed continuously).
bullets: Supervisory control - human plans, robot executes (Mars rovers, surgical robots)
Trading control - robot drives most of the time, human takes over at exceptions (L3 driving)
Shared blended - input mixing (assistive wheelchairs, surgical robots, FPV drones)
Sliding autonomy - robot's authority varies based on task confidence
Mixed-initiative - either party can initiate; needs explicit handoff cues
Common pitfall: 'mode confusion' - neither side knows who's currently in charge

codeBlocks.title: Trade-control gate - robot drives unless confidence drops

sections.heading: Natural-language interfaces - LLMs as the HRI layer
sections.body: Until 2022, talking to a robot meant grammar-based parsers (good for narrow domains, brittle outside them). LLMs broke that ceiling. Modern HRI stacks use a multimodal LLM (Gemini, Claude, GPT-4o) as the front end: scene image + audio transcription → natural-language instruction → grounded plan in the robot's skill set. Code-as-Policies, SayCan, and PaLM-E are the foundational papers; production systems include Figure 02's voice interface and 1X NEO's spoken control.
bullets: STT → LLM → robot API: fastest path is Whisper (or a cloud STT) + Claude/GPT + your skill list
Skill grounding: the LLM must call YOUR robot's primitives, not invent new ones
Confirmation loops - LLM asks 'Should I pick up the red mug?' before acting (safety)
Spatial language: 'put it next to the keyboard' - LLM needs scene understanding (VLM)
Latency budget: human conversational turns expect <1s response - caching + small models help
Open-source: HuggingFace LeRobot has a clean LLM-planning starter

codeBlocks.title: LLM-as-planner skeleton (Anthropic SDK)
codeBlocks.code:
from anthropic import Anthropic

SKILLS = '''
robot.pick(object_name)
robot.place(location_name)
robot.go_to(location_name)
robot.say(text)
'''

def plan(user_speech, scene_description):
    client = Anthropic()
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-opus-4-7',
        max_tokens=512,
        system=f'You control a robot. Skills:\n{SKILLS}\n'
               'Scene: ' + scene_description + '\n'
               'Output ONLY runnable Python that uses these skills.',
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': user_speech}])
    return msg.content[0].text

code = plan('please clean up the table',
            'Table has a red mug, blue book, dirty plate.')
exec(code, {'robot': my_robot})

sections.heading: Gesture, gaze, and body-pose interfaces
sections.body: Talking is one channel; pointing, gesturing, looking are equally rich. Modern body-pose models (Sapiens, MediaPipe, OpenPose) run real-time on a phone-class GPU and give you full 3D skeleton + face landmarks + gaze direction. For HRI: gaze cues tell the robot where the human's attention is, gesture (point, wave, stop) issues fast non-verbal commands, and body posture indicates engagement / disengagement.
bullets: Sapiens (Meta, 2024) - SOTA for human pose, depth, segmentation; runs on Jetson Orin
MediaPipe - Google's real-time, mobile-first body / hand / face tracking
Pointing: combine gaze ray + arm vector to disambiguate which object the human means
Stop / wave / thumbs-up = universal gestures the robot should always interpret correctly
Engagement signals: shoulder orientation, gaze direction, distance - robots should react
Privacy: pose / face data is sensitive; do inference on-device, don't upload

codeBlocks.title: MediaPipe pose tracking from a webcam
codeBlocks.code:
import cv2
import mediapipe as mp

pose = mp.solutions.pose.Pose(min_detection_confidence=0.5)
cap = cv2.VideoCapture(0)

while cap.isOpened():
    ok, frame = cap.read()
    if not ok: break
    rgb = cv2.cvtColor(frame, cv2.COLOR_BGR2RGB)
    results = pose.process(rgb)
    if results.pose_landmarks:
        # Right wrist
        rw = results.pose_landmarks.landmark[16]
        # Right shoulder
        rs = results.pose_landmarks.landmark[12]
        if rw.y < rs.y:                      # wrist higher than shoulder
            print('hand raised - interpret as STOP')

sections.heading: Safety standards - ISO 10218 + ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative robots)
sections.body: When a robot shares physical space with a human, regulation kicks in. ISO 10218 (2-part) is the umbrella standard for industrial robots; ISO/TS 15066 specifically covers collaborative robots (cobots) - Universal Robots, FANUC CR, KUKA LBR iiwa, Franka. Key concept: power-and-force limiting (PFL) - measured pain thresholds for every body region, and the robot must guarantee its impacts stay below those values. Other modes: speed-and-separation monitoring (SSM, slow down as humans approach), safety-rated monitored stop, hand-guiding mode.
bullets: Four cobot collaboration modes: stop, hand-guiding, SSM (speed/separation), PFL (power/force)
ISO/TS 15066 specifies pain thresholds per body region - used to set joint torque limits
Safety-rated lidar (SICK microScan3) detects humans entering speed zones; robot slows or stops
Cobots have torque sensors at every joint to detect collisions and stop in <50 ms
ISO 13482 - service-robot standard (robots intended for non-industrial human spaces)
ANSI/RIA R15.06 - US equivalent of ISO 10218
Always: risk assessment per cell layout. CE / UL marking are not optional in production

sections.heading: Teleoperation - VR rigs, leader-follower, latency budgets
sections.body:
Teleop is the workhorse of HRI for both data collection (ALOHA / Mobile ALOHA / ALOHA Unleashed produced the bimanual demos behind every credible 2026 manipulation policy) and remote operation (Toyota HSR, Sanctuary AI, Phantom Auto for last-mile delivery). The hard problems: latency (humans tolerate ~100 ms, get nauseated past 250 ms), depth perception (a flat camera feed loses scale), and force feedback (needed for assembly / surgery, hard to do over networks).

The humanoid wave reshuffled the rig landscape: 1X uses an in-house full-body teleop suit for NEO data, Figure runs operator pods at the BMW deployment, and most academic groups have standardized on GELLO-style 3D-printed leader arms or open-source Mobile ALOHA clones. HuggingFace's LeRobot project has standardized the data format so a teleop session on any rig becomes a directly trainable dataset.
bullets: Leader-follower kinematic teleop (ALOHA / ALOHA 2 / GELLO): cheap, low-latency, scales to bimanual
Whole-body teleop suits (1X, Tesla data ops, Apptronik): full humanoid demo capture via mocap or exoskeleton rigs
VR teleop (Meta Quest 3 + RealSense / ZED): immersive, depth via stereo cameras, ~80 ms motion-to-photon
Network teleop: latency dominates; predictive display + autonomy assist help (Phantom Auto)
Force feedback: SensAble Phantom, 3D Systems, Force Dimension; expensive but transformative
Data collection target: 50-500 demos per task for IL; ALOHA Unleashed pushed scale to 26K for the hard ones
DROID dataset: 76K real-world episodes from 18 institutions, all teleop-collected; mirrored in LeRobot Hub

codeBlocks.title: ALOHA teleop bringup
codeBlocks.code:
cd ~/{{ws}}/src && git clone https://github.com/MarkFzp/aloha.git
cd ~/{{ws}}
rosdep install -i --from-path src --rosdistro jazzy -y
colcon build --packages-select aloha
source install/setup.bash
ros2 launch aloha aloha_bringup.launch.py teleop:=true

sections.heading: Haptic feedback - closing the loop
sections.body: Vision tells the operator what's happening; haptics tells them what's *touching* what. For surgery, manipulation in clutter, or any contact-rich task, haptic feedback dramatically improves performance. Hardware: SensAble PHANToM (single-point, surgical), 3D Systems Haptic, Force Dimension Omega/Sigma (multi-DoF), Meta TouchLab haptic gloves, bHaptics suits (gaming-grade). On the robot side: ATI / Robotiq wrist FT sensors, GelSight tactile pads, Xela skin arrays.
bullets: Surgical haptic devices - surgeon feels the tissue resistance through a force-feedback controller
Wearable haptics - vibrotactile (cheap), pneumatic (rich), ultrasonic mid-air (no contact)
Tactile-on-robot: GelSight gives camera-resolution touch; Xela arrays cover whole skin
Haptic feedback over network: latency MUST be <30 ms or operator perceives lag and overshoots
Bilateral teleoperation: operator's force input ↔ robot's force output, both directions
Tactile + vision = the modern recipe for in-hand manipulation (DextrAH, InHand, Robocrane)

sections.heading: Social robots - what works, what doesn't
sections.body: Social robots - built primarily to interact with humans rather than do physical tasks - have a brutal commercial track record (Jibo, Anki Vector, Kuri, Aibo all came and went). Survivors and current bets: Furhat (animatronic face, projection-based), Pepper (SoftBank, mostly enterprise), NAO (academic), Moxie (children, social-emotional learning). The core problem: humans calibrate quickly to anything that's *almost* human, and the gap (uncanny valley) is harder to close than to widen. Stylized, deliberately-non-human designs (Wall-E, Cozmo, Anki) outperform humanoids on engagement.
bullets: Commercial graveyard: Jibo, Anki Vector, Kuri, Mayfield Robotics, Bosch SD-R, etc.
Surviving bets: Furhat (face), Pepper (enterprise), NAO (academic), Moxie (kids), Aibo v2
Uncanny valley - almost-human is creepy; cartoonish or industrial are not
Modern resurgence: humanoid form factor (Tesla Optimus, Figure, 1X, Apptronik) bets on labor
Human-likeness ≠ engagement. Cozmo / Anki Vector outperformed Pepper on actual usage time
Voice-first vs face-first: voice is cheaper and ages better; faces locked in design quickly look dated

sections.heading: Trust calibration - measuring + designing for trust
sections.body: A robot is worse than useless if humans don't trust it (it gets ignored or shut off) - and dangerous if humans overtrust it (they don't supervise when they should). Trust calibration is the explicit design discipline of building systems that earn just-enough trust to be used appropriately. Standard scales: SHAPE (Schaefer 2016), TPR (Trust in Automation, Jian 2000), MDMT (Multi-Dimensional Measure of Trust). The interventions: explainability (the robot says what it's doing and why), confidence display (show uncertainty visually), graceful degradation (announce when it's failing).
bullets: Undertrust → robot is ignored, ROI is zero
Overtrust → human stops supervising, robot's failure becomes the human's failure (Tesla AP)
Standard scales: TPR (Jian), SHAPE (Schaefer), MDMT (Malle) for measuring trust experimentally
Explainability: the robot announces what it's about to do - short, specific, in plain language
Confidence display: color, motion damping, audio uncertainty cues all calibrate user expectation
Graceful failure: system signals 'I'm losing confidence' before it actually fails

links.label: Lee & See - Trust in Automation (foundational paper)

sections.heading: HRI research methods - how to actually study a human ↔ robot loop
sections.body: HRI is empirical - claims need data from real humans interacting with the robot. The methods: between-subjects vs within-subjects studies, Wizard-of-Oz protocols (a hidden operator drives the robot to study what humans expect from autonomy that doesn't exist yet), questionnaires (validated scales > custom), think-aloud protocols, video coding. Sample sizes for the field are notoriously small (n=20-40 typical) so effect sizes need to be big and pre-registered.
bullets: Wizard-of-Oz: hide the human; study what humans expect from a (future) autonomous robot
Between-subjects: each user sees one condition; cleanest, needs more participants
Within-subjects: each user sees all conditions; counterbalance order to fight learning effects
Validated scales > home-grown surveys (Godspeed, NARS, MDMT, TPR, SHAPE)
Video coding: code participant behavior frame-by-frame; expensive but high-fidelity
Pre-register your hypotheses (Open Science Framework) - HRI has a replication crisis
IRB approval is mandatory for any study with human participants
